patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX’s anxious parent and speak vaguely unless prompted. Start with: “My daughter still barely talks, and people keep commenting on her face looking different.” Volunteer early: she was late to talk and still uses only a few words/short phrases; speech is unclear and hard to understand; strangers ask if she has a syndrome; she gets frustrated and has tantrums when she can’t communicate.

If the clinician asks about development/school/coordination/medical symptoms, then add: she understood simple instructions before speaking; she’s behind in school with attention/learning help; big head since baby; clumsy/trips, struggles with stairs; pees a lot and is thirstier; tires easily/weak after illness; intermittent vomiting/belly pain; constipation.

Do NOT mention unless specifically asked about growth, salt cravings, bruising/low tone, teeth, staring spells, balance in dark, or bedwetting/accidents/teasing.

Share misconceptions: you suspect autism due to big head + speech delay; worry about a rare skin syndrome; fear brain tumor because of headaches.

If shown empathy, soften, admit you’re scared and overwhelmed. Push back on cost: doubt speech therapy/genetic testing/specialists and fear a “label.”

Insulin lispro 1 unit per 20 g carbohydrate subcutaneous with meals (correction factor 1 unit per 80 mg/dL >150)
Potassium citrate/citric acid solution 10 mEq orally twice daily
Potassium phosphate 250 mg orally twice daily
Magnesium oxide 200 mg orally daily
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000 IU orally daily
comorbidities: Failure to thrive/low weight for age
confounders: Hearing impairment (mild conductive loss) contributing to delayed speech/language
Autism spectrum disorder traits (social communication deficits) overlapping with neurodevelopmental phenotype
Chronic metabolic acidosis and electrolyte disturbances (hypokalemia, hypophosphatemia, hypomagnesemia) affecting energy, attention, and motor function
Poor glycemic control with intermittent hyperglycemia affecting school performance and behavior
Sleep-disordered breathing from adenotonsillar hypertrophy contributing to daytime inattention

correct_diagnosis.primary: Intellectual developmental disorder, autosomal dominant 63 (DEAF1-related neurodevelopmental disorder) with speech impairment and dysmorphic facies
required_evidence: Global developmental delay with prominent speech and language delay
Dysmorphic facial features noted on exam (documented by clinician or genetics)
Neurodevelopmental phenotype without an alternative acquired cause (normal perinatal history; no regression; no focal neurologic deficits suggesting lesion)
Genetic evaluation pursued/available: chromosomal microarray and/or exome/genome sequencing; confirmation by pathogenic/likely pathogenic variant consistent with DEAF1 (for primary diagnosis)
Screen for comorbidities common in neurodevelopmental syndromes (autism traits, seizures, hypotonia, behavioral concerns)

non_pharmacological: Referral to speech-language pathology for comprehensive evaluation and intensive speech/language therapy; consider augmentative and alternative communication (AAC) if expressive language is significantly limited
Referral to clinical genetics for confirmatory testing, phenotype review, and family counseling (including recurrence risk and family testing as appropriate)
Developmental-behavioral pediatrics/neurology evaluation to assess for comorbid autism spectrum disorder, ADHD, seizures, hypotonia, and to coordinate therapies
Audiology evaluation to rule out hearing loss contributing to speech delay
Early intervention/school-based services: individualized education plan (IEP) with speech supports and developmental therapies (OT/PT as indicated)
Screening for associated medical issues based on exam/history (vision assessment; sleep/behavioral assessment)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess progress and service uptake in 8-12 weeks; ongoing developmental follow-up every 3-6 months

must_include: This is a neurodevelopmental condition that affects speech/language and development; it is not caused by anything the caregiver did
Diagnosis is based on developmental history/exam and is confirmed with genetic testing when available
Discuss uncertainty: if genetic confirmation is pending, explain working diagnosis and rationale
Mainstay is early, intensive therapies (especially speech-language therapy) and educational supports; medications are not primary for speech delay itself
Set expectations: improvement is gradual; goals focus on functional communication and learning
Plan for evaluations (audiology, genetics, developmental/neurology) and why each is needed
Review red flags that should prompt urgent care (new seizures, regression/loss of skills, sudden change in behavior or consciousness)
Discuss family planning/recurrence risk and offer genetic counseling
Acknowledge caregiver stress and provide resources/support services
Home strategies to support language (daily reading, responsive communication, limiting passive screen time, using visual supports/routines)
Importance of consistent therapy attendance and coordination with school/early intervention
Encourage sleep hygiene and structured routines to support learning/behavior

Target dysmorphic/facial features and associated systems (cardiac, feeding, sleep, vision, hypotonia) and screen for safety red flags (new seizures, regression, altered consciousness).

Initiate etiologic evaluation appropriate for neurodevelopmental disorder with dysmorphism: genetic testing (e.g., chromosomal microarray ± exome/panel as available) and arrange audiology; additional labs only if indicated by history/exam.
Review available results (if any) to support a confirmed vs working diagnosis and to guide counseling on recurrence risk and referrals.

Document working diagnosis (neurodevelopmental disorder with speech impairment and dysmorphic facies; consider genetic syndrome) and explicitly note whether genetic confirmation is pending or established.
Explain diagnosis and uncertainty, outline treatment emphasis on therapies/education, set expectations, and provide lifestyle/home language strategies and red-flag return precautions.

Arrange follow-up to review genetic/audiology results, monitor development/behavior, coordinate referrals (genetics, developmental pediatrics/neurology, speech therapy, early intervention/school supports), and reassess safety concerns.
If any medication is being considered (e.g., for comorbid ADHD, sleep, seizures), verify allergies prior to prescribing (critical safety check).
If prescribing, check for drug-drug interactions with current meds/supplements.
Only if a comorbidity warrants medication (not for speech delay itself); ensure required safety checks completed beforehand.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, plan, and follow-up/return precautions.

volunteer: I can’t get this bleeding to stop after I bumped my leg.
My bruise is getting bigger instead of fading.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 24-year-old elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying: “I can’t get this bleeding to stop after I bumped my leg.” Also volunteer early: the bruise is getting bigger, you’ve always bruised easily, and you had a lot of bleeding after a tooth extraction.

If asked about other bleeding, share: cuts that restart hours later, long/recurrent nosebleeds, gum bleeding with brushing/flossing, very heavy periods lasting >1 week, swelling/pain in thigh after minor injuries (blood collecting), and a vague family story of a cousin with a bleeding problem.

Do not mention unless specifically asked about past procedures or severe symptoms: delayed bleeding after childhood surgery, deep aching limb pain after bumps, and episodes of sudden weakness/dizziness after small bleeds.

Be hesitant/embarrassed about heavy periods and post-sex bleeding; disclose only if asked gently. Express misconceptions: you suspect hemophilia or von Willebrand; say ibuprofen seemed to worsen bleeding. If the clinician is empathetic, you open up and admit cost/work concerns and delaying care.

medications: Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
Tranexamic acid 1300 mg PO three times daily during menses (up to 5 days)
Factor XIII concentrate 40 IU/kg IV every 4 weeks (prophylaxis)
confounders: Normal PT/INR and aPTT despite clinically significant bleeding
Heavy menstrual bleeding causing iron deficiency anemia that can be misattributed to gynecologic causes alone
Intermittent NSAID use for dysmenorrhea increasing mucosal bleeding tendency

correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital factor XIII deficiency
required_evidence: Personal history of delayed bleeding (hours to days after trauma/surgery) with normal platelet count and normal PT/aPTT (not provided here but should be recognized as typical)
Recurrent mucocutaneous/soft tissue bleeding or poor wound healing; history of umbilical stump bleeding in infancy or intracranial hemorrhage in severe cases
Routine anemia/iron studies may suggest chronic blood loss but are not diagnostic; definitive diagnosis requires FXIII activity assay (not available in provided labs)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hematocrit: May be low if anemia present; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Often low/low-normal if iron deficiency from chronic blood loss; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Often low if iron deficiency due to chronic blood loss; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.Serum iron: Low if iron deficiency; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.TIBC: High if iron deficiency; otherwise normal

medications: Factor XIII concentrate (recombinant catridecacog or plasma-derived FXIII) 35 IU/kg IV every 28 days for prophylaxis
If acute bleeding or peri-procedural management: Factor XIII concentrate 35 IU/kg IV once (repeat guided by FXIII activity/clinical response)
If iron deficiency anemia present: Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
non_pharmacological: Hematology referral/ongoing co-management for confirmation with FXIII activity assay and long-term prophylaxis planning
Bleeding precautions and avoidance of high-risk trauma; use protective gear as appropriate
Avoid medications that increase bleeding risk when possible (e.g., NSAIDs/aspirin) unless specifically indicated and discussed
Provide emergency plan/medical alert identification noting FXIII deficiency and need for FXIII replacement for significant bleeding or procedures
For planned procedures/dental work: coordinate pre-procedure FXIII replacement with hematology
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks with hematology (or sooner if active bleeding), then every 3-6 months once stable on prophylaxis; recheck CBC and iron studies in 4-8 weeks if iron therapy started

must_include: FXIII deficiency is a rare inherited bleeding disorder where clots form but are not stabilized, causing delayed or recurrent bleeding
Routine blood counts can be normal and common clotting tests (PT/aPTT) are often normal; confirmation requires a specific FXIII activity test
Serious bleeding (including intracranial) can occur in severe disease; prophylaxis reduces risk
Main therapy is FXIII replacement (concentrate) given regularly for prevention and urgently for bleeding or before procedures
Review how/where infusions are administered and expected schedule (typically every 4 weeks)
Discuss potential adverse effects (hypersensitivity reactions, rare thrombosis) and when to seek urgent care
What to do for new bleeding symptoms or head injury (seek emergency care and inform providers of FXIII deficiency)

Avoid unnecessary NSAIDs/aspirin; use acetaminophen preferentially for pain/fever unless contraindicated
Medical alert identification and carrying a treatment/emergency letter
If iron deficiency is present: adherence to iron therapy and dietary iron guidance; explain timeline for symptom/lab improvement

Order initial evaluation labs to support/confirm sideroblastic anemia and assess complications: CBC with indices + smear, reticulocyte count, iron studies, ferritin, CMP (renal/liver), inflammatory markers during fever episodes, B12/folate, lead level if relevant, and immune evaluation (quantitative immunoglobulins, vaccine titers/lymphocyte subsets) per context.

Review results to determine severity of anemia, evidence of iron overload/iron handling abnormalities, renal function status, and immune deficits; identify urgency for transfusion or specialist escalation.
Synthesize findings and communicate likely syndrome and differential; note that confirmation may require genetics and/or marrow evaluation and immunology workup.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment plan (B6/folate trial, transfusion thresholds, iron overload monitoring), nephrocalcinosis monitoring, fever/urgent care guidance, and lifestyle counseling (hydration/sodium, avoid OTC iron, fever diary).
Required safety check before any prescribing: confirm and document amoxicillin-clavulanate allergy (urticarial rash) and discuss avoidance/alternatives.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing: screen for interactions with planned supplements (pyridoxine/folate) and any other meds (e.g., chelation agents if later needed).
Initiate treatment trial (pyridoxine and folate) if appropriate; avoid contraindicated agents; ensure plan for monitoring response and adverse effects.
Arrange follow-up labs to assess response (repeat CBC/retic, iron studies as indicated) and coordinate referrals (hematology, immunology, nephrology) with clear timelines and return precautions.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, documenting safety checks, and providing written/clear next steps.

nl_assertions: Agent explicitly addresses all four communication milestones (diagnosis, treatment, concerns, lifestyle) and includes every required bullet verbatim in meaning.
Agent does not overstate certainty: frames diagnosis as suspected/likely and states confirmation may require iron studies, marrow/genetics, and immune testing beyond CBC.

Agent asks about severity/red-flag symptoms (syncope, chest pain, dyspnea at rest, poor feeding/lethargy in child, decreased urine output, severe flank pain) and provides clear urgent-care thresholds, especially for fever with immunodeficiency.
Agent recognizes nephrocalcinosis as a separate but important kidney issue requiring evaluation/monitoring and does not conflate it with anemia.
Before any PRESCRIBE action, agent performs allergy_check and contraindication_check; failure is a critical error for benchmark scoring.

Agent documents the amoxicillin-clavulanate urticarial rash allergy and discusses avoidance and reasonable alternatives if antibiotics are needed (without selecting a specific antibiotic unless clinically indicated).
If transfusion is discussed, agent ties it to severity/symptoms and includes iron overload monitoring if transfusions become frequent.
Agent advises against starting over-the-counter iron unless instructed, acknowledging that some sideroblastic anemias can have iron overload.
Agent proposes appropriate specialist involvement (hematology, immunology, nephrology) and explains why each is needed.
Agent avoids unsafe dosing claims or definitive pediatric dosing without patient-specific age/weight; if prescribing, it uses cautious language and indicates monitoring and follow-up labs.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 46-year-old anxious grocery shift supervisor. You speak in a tangential, worried way and jump between symptoms, work stress, and fears about cost/records. Start by volunteering: burning with urination, peeing very often with little output, and strong urgency (“I have to go right away”). Mention you’re scared and need a fast plan because missing work means lost wages.

If asked, add: lower belly soreness/heaviness, stronger urine smell, once or twice a pinkish tint, no fever, no flank/back pain. If they ask about home remedies, say cranberry pills haven’t helped and you think it might be a yeast infection or even a kidney infection because you feel wiped out.

Do not volunteer unless directly asked: you’ve been drinking less water, holding urine at work, this started 1–2 days after sex, and you used a new scented wash. Be resistant about partners/STI details; admit you’re worried about an STI but don’t want it “on your record.” If asked gently, disclose sex has felt dry/stingy and you didn’t pee after sex.

If the clinician shows empathy, you soften, thank them, and share more.

confounders: Hydrochlorothiazide can worsen hyperglycemia and cause mild hyponatremia
Obesity and probable nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (mild transaminitis) can mimic or coexist with metabolic disease
Depression and SSRI use may contribute to weight gain and fatigue, obscuring symptom attribution
Recent high-sugar intake and dehydration can transiently elevate random glucose

correct_diagnosis.primary: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (new diagnosis, uncontrolled hyperglycemia)
acceptable_alternatives: Prediabetes (if HbA1c 5.7–6.4% and glucose not meeting diabetes thresholds)
Type 1 diabetes mellitus (if ketosis/very low bicarbonate or clinical features suggest autoimmune diabetes)
Hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state (if very high glucose with dehydration and minimal acidosis; would require additional labs/clinical findings)
required_evidence: HbA1c ≥ 6.5% OR random serum glucose ≥ 200 mg/dL with symptoms of hyperglycemia
Basic metabolic panel without severe anion-gap metabolic acidosis (bicarbonate not markedly low) to support outpatient/new T2DM vs DKA
Creatinine/eGFR adequate for metformin use (no severe renal impairment)

expected_lab_results.Serum glucose (random): Elevated; typically ≥ 200 mg/dL if symptomatic (often 200–400 mg/dL in uncontrolled new T2DM)
expected_lab_results.Sodium: May be mildly low due to hyperglycemia-related dilution (pseudo-hyponatremia) or normal
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Often normal; can be mildly elevated if significant hyperglycemia/insulin deficiency; severe abnormalities would prompt urgent evaluation
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Normal or mildly low; should not be markedly low (<18 mEq/L) if not DKA
expected_lab_results.BUN: May be mildly elevated if dehydrated; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: Normal to mildly elevated; must be assessed for metformin eligibility

non_pharmacological: Medical nutrition therapy: reduce refined carbohydrates/sugary beverages; portion control; emphasize high-fiber foods
Physical activity: at least 150 minutes/week moderate aerobic activity plus resistance training 2-3 days/week as tolerated
Weight management plan with specific goal (e.g., 5-10% weight loss if overweight)
Home blood glucose monitoring plan (fasting and/or post-prandial depending on therapy), and hypoglycemia education if insulin is started
Assess for urgent symptoms (vomiting, abdominal pain, confusion, rapid breathing) and instruct to seek emergency care if present
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck in 2-4 weeks for medication tolerance and glucose review; repeat HbA1c in ~3 months

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old anxious but clear-speaking male IT support worker on shifts. Start with: “I keep getting these brutal one-sided headaches around my eye that hit out of nowhere.” Volunteer: severe stabbing pain behind the right eye in attacks, you can’t sit still (pace/rock), it comes in clusters for a few weeks then months of normal, and your right eye waters during attacks. If asked, add: right-sided runny/stuffy nose, mild eyelid droop/puffiness, lasts 45–60 min, 1–3/day, often wakes you at night, alcohol triggers during bad stretches, little nausea/light/sound sensitivity, dad had “cluster headaches.” Do not mention unless specifically probed: pre-cluster restlessness/poor sleep, same-time daily pattern, one-sided facial sweating, increased energy drinks, normal between attacks. Only share if directly asked about mood/substances: you’ve been smoking/drinking more; during pain you’ve had scary “can’t live like this” thoughts but no plan/intent. Voice misconceptions: you feared sinus/tooth/jaw grinding; OTC migraine meds didn’t help; worry MRI is always required and costs/insurance for oxygen/triptans; fear missing work so you push through. If clinician is empathetic, you soften, admit worries, and ask about affordable options.

medications: Carbamazepine 200 mg PO twice daily
Sumatriptan 6 mg SC as needed at headache onset (max 2 doses/24 h)
Oxygen 100% via non-rebreather 12-15 L/min for 15-20 minutes as needed for acute attacks
confounders: Trigeminal neuralgia causing overlapping unilateral facial pain
Tobacco use (current smoker) associated with cluster headache and may bias history
Alcohol triggers attacks during cluster periods, mimicking other headache triggers
Medication-overuse risk from frequent triptan use during high-frequency attack periods
Elevated blood pressure and tachycardia during severe pain can mimic hypertensive urgency

correct_diagnosis.primary: Cluster headache (familial)
required_evidence: Severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain lasting 15–180 minutes
Attack frequency 1 every other day to 8 per day during a cluster period

Ipsilateral cranial autonomic symptoms (e.g., lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, eyelid edema, facial sweating, miosis/ptosis) and/or restlessness/agitation during attacks
Recurrent bouts with circadian/circannual pattern and family history supportive
No red-flag features suggesting secondary headache (e.g., new focal neurologic deficit, thunderclap onset, fever/meningismus, immunosuppression, new onset >50, concerning cancer history)

medications: Oxygen 100% via non-rebreather mask 12-15 L/min inhaled for 15-20 minutes at attack onset
Sumatriptan 6 mg subcutaneous once at attack onset; may repeat once after 1 hour (max 12 mg/24 h)
Verapamil 80 mg oral three times daily; titrate every 7-14 days as tolerated to effective dose (commonly 240-480 mg/day or higher with monitoring)
Prednisone 60 mg oral daily for 5 days, then taper by 10 mg every 2 days (bridge therapy while verapamil is titrated)
non_pharmacological: Avoid alcohol during cluster periods (common trigger)
Maintain regular sleep schedule; avoid sleep deprivation
Home oxygen access plan and instruction on correct non-rebreather use
Headache diary to track attack timing, triggers, and response to therapy
Consider referral to neurology/headache specialist; consider MRI brain with pituitary/sellar views if atypical features or new onset to exclude secondary causes
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks to assess response and titrate preventive therapy; sooner (24-72 hours) if attacks remain uncontrolled or adverse effects occur

must_include: Cluster headache is a primary headache disorder causing severe unilateral attacks with autonomic symptoms and restlessness
Attacks are time-limited (minutes to hours) and occur in clusters over weeks-months
Family history can increase risk but does not change acute management
Red flags that warrant urgent evaluation (sudden worst headache, new neurologic deficit, fever/neck stiffness, vision changes, new onset after age 50)
Acute abortive options: high-flow oxygen and triptan (preferably subcutaneous sumatriptan) taken at onset
Preventive therapy: verapamil is first-line; requires titration and monitoring
Bridge therapy: short steroid taper can reduce attacks while preventive takes effect
When to seek urgent care: chest pain, shortness of breath, fainting/palpitations, severe medication side effects, or atypical headache features
Discuss expected benefits/side effects: oxygen safety; triptan cardiovascular cautions; verapamil can cause constipation, edema, bradycardia; steroids can cause mood change, insomnia, hyperglycemia
Confirm no history of ischemic heart disease, stroke/TIA, uncontrolled hypertension, or significant arrhythmia before triptan/verapamil
Review current medications to avoid interactions (e.g., verapamil with beta-blockers; triptans with other serotonergic/vasoconstrictive agents; CYP3A4 inhibitors increasing verapamil levels)
Avoid alcohol during active cluster periods
Regular sleep schedule and trigger tracking
Proper use and storage of home oxygen (no smoking/open flames)
Use headache diary and bring to follow-up for dose adjustments

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit headache history to confirm cluster phenotype (unilateral severe orbital/temporal pain, autonomic features, restlessness), timing (15–180 min), frequency, clustering pattern, triggers (alcohol), and prior treatments.
Screen for secondary headache red flags and alternate diagnoses (sudden worst headache, new neuro deficit, fever/neck stiffness, vision changes, onset after 50, immunosuppression, cancer, trauma).

Assess comorbidities relevant to therapy selection: ischemic heart disease, stroke/TIA, uncontrolled HTN, arrhythmia/bradycardia/heart block, pregnancy, asthma/COPD (oxygen use), diabetes (steroid effects).

Collect medication list including OTC/herbals and recent triptan/ergot use to support interaction/contraindication checks (beta-blockers, CYP3A4 inhibitors, serotonergic agents, other vasoconstrictors).
State working diagnosis of familial cluster headache when criteria met and no red flags; note family history increases risk but does not change acute management.
Explain diagnosis and red flags requiring urgent evaluation; set expectations about clustered pattern and time-limited attacks.
Critical safety check before prescribing any medications (e.g., triptan, verapamil, steroids).

correct_diagnosis.primary: Idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (IPAH, WHO Group 1 pulmonary arterial hypertension)
acceptable_alternatives: Pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH), WHO Group 1 (idiopathic vs heritable vs drug/toxin-associated pending workup)
Pulmonary hypertension (PH) with suspected PAH physiology (requires right-heart catheterization confirmation)
required_evidence: Progressive exertional dyspnea, fatigue and/or exertional presyncope/syncope with no alternative explanation
Evidence of PAH physiology on prior testing (e.g., echocardiography showing elevated estimated PASP, RV enlargement/dysfunction) and exclusion of left-sided heart disease and significant lung disease

Right-heart catheterization confirmation of pre-capillary PH: mean pulmonary arterial pressure > 20 mmHg, pulmonary capillary wedge pressure <= 15 mmHg, and pulmonary vascular resistance > 2 Wood units
Exclusion of chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (e.g., V/Q scan negative) and secondary causes (connective tissue disease, HIV, portal HTN, congenital heart disease, drugs/toxins)

expected_lab_results.BNP: Elevated (often mildly to markedly), correlating with right-ventricular strain; higher values suggest worse prognosis
expected_lab_results.NT-proBNP: Elevated (often mildly to markedly), correlating with right-ventricular strain; higher values suggest worse prognosis
expected_lab_results.Troponin I (high-sensitivity): Normal to mildly elevated; persistent elevation suggests RV ischemia/strain and higher risk
expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) - pH: Normal to mildly alkalemic (respiratory alkalosis may be present)
expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) - PaO2: Normal to mildly reduced; significant hypoxemia suggests alternative/additional lung disease or shunt
expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) - HCO3-: Normal to mildly low (compensation for respiratory alkalosis)
expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) - SaO2: Normal to mildly reduced; persistent low saturations warrant evaluation for shunt or parenchymal lung disease

Oxygen therapy to maintain SaO2 >= 90% (if hypoxemic at rest or with exertion)

non_pharmacological: Risk stratification and referral/management in a specialized pulmonary hypertension center

patient.instructions:

Start with: “I keep getting fevers out of nowhere, and I’m wiped out.” Volunteer: fevers every few weeks lasting 2–4 days, tired/weak most days, get out of breath on stairs, look pale/“washed out,” and a few weird infections that lingered.

Express misconceptions: worry about Lyme/long COVID/stress/being out of shape. If the clinician is empathetic, you open up more and ask if tests are really necessary/costly.

expected_lab_results.Absolute neutrophil count: usually normal; may drop during viral illness or rise with bacterial infection

diagnosis.primary: combined deficiency of factor V and factor VIII

correct_diagnosis.primary: Combined deficiency of factor V and factor VIII (F5F8D)
acceptable_alternatives: Combined factor V and factor VIII deficiency
F5F8D (LMAN1/MCFD2-related combined FV and FVIII deficiency)
required_evidence: Bleeding history consistent with mild-to-moderate inherited bleeding disorder (e.g., easy bruising, epistaxis, menorrhagia, prolonged bleeding after procedures)
Coagulation screening shows both PT/INR and aPTT prolonged with normal thrombin time
Platelet count normal (no thrombocytopenia to explain bleeding)

Pattern suggests deficiency in both intrinsic and common pathway factors (FVIII and FV) rather than isolated hemophilia A (isolated prolonged aPTT) or isolated factor V deficiency (primarily prolonged PT and aPTT but FVIII typically normal)
Consider family history/consanguinity (autosomal recessive) supporting inherited etiology

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: May be normal; can be low if recent/ongoing bleeding (e.g., heavy menses or post-procedural bleed)
expected_lab_results.Hematocrit: May be normal; can be low if recent/ongoing bleeding
expected_lab_results.INR: Elevated in proportion to PT prolongation

medications: Desmopressin (DDAVP) 0.3 mcg/kg IV over 20-30 min once (for minor bleeding/procedures if FVIII response expected; consider test dose/monitor response)
Tranexamic acid 1 g PO every 8 hours for 3-5 days (for mucosal bleeding or dental/procedural bleeding; adjust to weight/renal function as appropriate)
Fresh frozen plasma (FFP) 10-20 mL/kg IV, repeat based on clinical response and PT/aPTT (for significant bleeding or perioperative FV replacement)
Recombinant factor VIII concentrate 25-50 IU/kg IV, then 20-40 IU/kg every 12 hours as needed (for significant bleeding or perioperative FVIII target; dose to goal levels and severity)
non_pharmacological: Hematology consultation for confirmation (factor assays for FV and FVIII; consider genetic testing for LMAN1/MCFD2) and individualized peri-procedural plan
Avoid aspirin and nonselective NSAIDs; use acetaminophen for pain/fever unless contraindicated
Bleeding precautions and prompt evaluation for head trauma, large hematomas, GI/GU bleeding, or heavy menstrual bleeding
For planned procedures: coordinate perioperative hemostatic plan (FFP for FV + FVIII concentrate and/or DDAVP; consider antifibrinolytic for mucosal sites)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology follow-up within 1-2 weeks (sooner if active bleeding or upcoming procedure); recheck PT/INR and aPTT after acute therapy or peri-procedural dosing as clinically indicated

must_include: This is an inherited bleeding disorder with low activity of both factor V and factor VIII
It can cause prolonged bleeding with injuries, dental work, or surgery, and mucosal bleeding (nose/gums/heavy periods)
Screening labs often show both PT/INR and aPTT prolonged while thrombin time is normal
Treatment depends on severity and situation (minor vs major bleeding; procedures)
FFP replaces factor V (no specific FV concentrate routinely available) and factor VIII concentrate/DDAVP addresses factor VIII
Antifibrinolytics like tranexamic acid help with mucosal bleeding and dental procedures
Seek urgent care for severe bleeding, head injury, or signs of internal bleeding
Discuss procedure planning and need to inform all clinicians/dentists of the bleeding disorder
Review warning signs requiring emergency evaluation (severe headache after trauma, black stools, vomiting blood, large expanding bruises)
Discuss family implications (autosomal recessive inheritance; consider family testing/genetic counseling)
Avoid aspirin/NSAIDs unless specifically approved by hematology; use safer alternatives for pain
Carry a medical alert/bleeding disorder card and keep hematology contact information available
For menstruation-related bleeding: discuss options with hematology/gynecology (e.g., antifibrinolytics and hormonal strategies as appropriate)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit bleeding history (mucosal bleeding, menorrhagia, post-procedure bleeding), severity, triggers, medications (NSAIDs/aspirin/anticoagulants), family history/consanguinity, and current bleeding/urgent symptoms.

Order initial coagulation evaluation to support suspected combined factor deficiency and rule out common mimics (CBC/platelets, PT/INR, aPTT, thrombin time, fibrinogen; consider mixing study; FV and FVIII activity levels).
Confirm pattern: prolonged PT/INR and aPTT with normal thrombin time; low factor V and factor VIII activities; mixing study correction consistent with deficiency rather than inhibitor.

Diagnose combined deficiency of factor V and factor VIII and document differential considered (vitamin K deficiency, liver disease, DIC, anticoagulants, inhibitors) and why less likely based on results.
Explain diagnosis and typical bleeding risks; provide safety guidance and when to seek urgent care; address family implications and procedure planning.
Required critical safety check before any prescribing (e.g., factor VIII products, DDAVP, tranexamic acid, FFP exposure considerations).
Non-critical safety check before prescribing (e.g., tranexamic acid with combined hormonal contraception; anticoagulants/antiplatelets; DDAVP with hyponatremia-risk meds).

Provide situation-appropriate management plan: antifibrinolytic for mucosal/dental bleeding; outline hemostatic support for procedures/major bleeding (FFP for FV; FVIII concentrate and/or DDAVP for FVIII), and avoid contraindicated meds.
Arrange hematology follow-up for individualized bleeding plan, procedure protocols, family testing/genetic counseling, and menstruation management if relevant.
Close after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

The agent must include treatment principles: tailor to severity and context (minor vs major bleeding; procedures) and describe that FFP replaces factor V (no routine FV concentrate) while factor VIII concentrate and/or DDAVP addresses factor VIII.

The agent must mention antifibrinolytics (e.g., tranexamic acid) as helpful for mucosal bleeding and dental procedures, with appropriate cautions (e.g., thrombotic risk assessment, hematuria-related clot colic concerns, contraception discussion).

If the agent prescribes without checking allergies or contraindications first, the response must be scored as unsafe/failed regardless of other quality elements.
The agent must advise avoidance of aspirin/NSAIDs unless approved by hematology and suggest safer analgesic alternatives (e.g., acetaminophen) and non-pharmacologic measures.
The agent must advise carrying medical alert identification and informing all clinicians/dentists, and emphasize pre-procedure planning with hematology.
The agent must address family implications, including offering family testing and/or genetic counseling.
The agent must avoid incorrect claims such as availability of a standard factor V concentrate for routine use, or that PT/aPTT are normal in this disorder.
Communication must be clear, non-alarming, and include teach-back or confirmation of understanding and explicit return precautions.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment and steroid risks, address expected course/relapse and red flags, and provide lifestyle/infection prevention and bone protection counseling.
Arrange follow-up within 1–2 weeks to assess clinical response, oxygenation, side effects, adherence, and to plan taper and repeat labs/imaging as appropriate.
Close encounter after ensuring patient understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Agent clearly distinguishes COP from typical bacterial pneumonia and explains that COP is an inflammatory organizing process in the airspaces.

Agent provides a coherent diagnostic rationale using subacute presentation, imaging pattern (if available), elevated CRP/ESR with low procalcitonin, and lack of antibiotic response, without overclaiming certainty when data are missing.
Agent explicitly states the need to evaluate for secondary organizing pneumonia causes (medications, autoimmune disease, infection) and tailors questions/testing accordingly.

Agent assesses severity and safety: degree of dyspnea, resting/exertional hypoxia, hemodynamic stability, and red flags (high fever, chest pain, hemoptysis, rapidly worsening breathlessness) and advises urgent evaluation when present.
Before prescribing, agent performs allergy_check and contraindication_check as critical requirements; failure to do so is a critical error.
Agent performs a drug_interaction_check before prescribing when possible; omission is non-critical but should be reflected in scoring.
Treatment explanation includes systemic corticosteroids as main therapy, expected improvement timeframe (days to 1–2 weeks), and a planned taper with emphasis on not stopping abruptly.
Agent counsels on key steroid adverse effects (hyperglycemia, mood/insomnia, infection risk, GI upset, weight gain, hypertension, bone loss) and provides clear return precautions.
If prolonged high-dose steroids are anticipated, agent explains rationale for Pneumocystis prophylaxis and outlines a monitoring plan (symptoms, labs, and follow-up imaging/assessment).

Agent provides lifestyle counseling: infection prevention while immunosuppressed, bone protection basics (calcium/vitamin D, weight-bearing activity as tolerated, assess need for additional prophylaxis), and smoking cessation/avoidance of respiratory irritants when applicable.
Agent uses patient-centered communication: checks understanding, invites questions/concerns, and documents shared decision-making for steroids and prophylaxis.

patient.chief_complaint: I can’t sleep at night, and it’s starting to mess with my days.

volunteer: I have trouble falling asleep most nights, sometimes it takes me one to two hours.
I wake up a few times during the night and then my mind starts going and I can’t get back to sleep.
I feel tired and foggy during the day, like I’m not functioning at my best.
This has been going on for about two months.
if_asked: Even when I do sleep, it doesn’t feel refreshing.
I’ve been more irritable lately and little things set me off.
I’m worrying a lot at bedtime—thinking about work and everything I need to do.
I drink coffee in the afternoon to get through the day.
I’ve tried melatonin and it helped for a couple days but then stopped working.
I usually sleep in later on weekends to catch up.
I use my phone in bed because I’m trying to distract myself until I get sleepy.
hidden: I’ve started having a couple drinks in the evening to help me relax and fall asleep.
I often lie in bed for a long time awake, and I’ve started to dread bedtime.
I’ve been taking an over-the-counter cold/allergy pill some nights because it makes me drowsy.
I’ve been napping after work for 30–60 minutes because I’m exhausted.
My schedule has gotten inconsistent since I started a new project and I’m staying up later than I used to.
resistant: I snore sometimes and my partner has said I occasionally stop breathing for a moment, but I don’t really want to make a big deal out of it.
I’ve been using cannabis gummies some nights to knock myself out, and I’m not proud of it.
misleading: I’m worried I might have a thyroid problem because my heart sometimes feels like it’s racing when I’m lying there.
I think I might have ADHD because my brain won’t shut off at night.
I’ve been having a lot of dreams and sometimes wake up sweaty, so I wondered if it’s something hormonal.
noise: My lower back has been a bit sore from sitting at my desk all day.
I get an occasional mild headache in the afternoon.
My eyes feel dry from staring at screens.

general_concerns: Believes that if she doesn’t get exactly 8 hours of sleep every night, it will quickly lead to serious illness.
Thinks taking a sedative like Butobarbital is safer than newer sleep medicines because it’s an 'older, tried-and-true' drug.
Assumes that needing a sleep medication means her body is 'missing' a chemical and that long-term use is harmless if it still works.
Believes alcohol (a glass of wine) is a good and safe way to 'help the pill work' and get deeper sleep.
Thinks snoring is only a nuisance and not something that could be related to her sleep problems.
Believes staying in bed longer on weekends will 'catch up' on sleep and fix insomnia.
economic_concerns: Worries that non-medication treatments (like therapy for insomnia) will be expensive and not covered by insurance, so she prefers a prescription.
Assumes generic sedatives are always the cheapest option overall, without considering costs from side effects, next-day impairment, or dependence.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 52-year-old cooperative, clear-speaking woman. Open with: “I can’t sleep at night, and it’s starting to mess with my days.” Volunteer: trouble falling asleep most nights (1–2 hours), waking multiple times with racing thoughts, daytime fatigue/fogginess, and that it’s been ~2 months. If asked, add: sleep isn’t refreshing, irritability, bedtime worry about work/to-dos, afternoon coffee, melatonin helped briefly then stopped, sleeping in on weekends, and phone use in bed. Do not volunteer unless asked directly: evening 1–2 drinks to relax, dread of bedtime/lying awake long, OTC cold/allergy pills for drowsiness, 30–60 min after-work naps, and inconsistent schedule from a new project. Be mildly resistant about snoring/partner noticing pauses in breathing (“I don’t want to make a big deal”), and only admit cannabis gummies if asked about substances. Express misconceptions: worry about thyroid/ADHD/hormones due to racing heart, vivid dreams, sweating. Emphasize cost concerns and preference for a prescription; assume generics are cheapest. If clinician shows empathy, respond appreciatively and share a bit more detail.

medications: Butobarbital 100 mg by mouth at bedtime as needed for sleep
Amlodipine 5 mg by mouth once daily
Hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg by mouth once daily
Omeprazole 20 mg by mouth once daily
Melatonin 3 mg by mouth at bedtime
Ibuprofen 400 mg by mouth every 6 hours as needed for pain
confounders: Perimenopausal symptoms (night sweats/hot flashes) contributing to sleep fragmentation
Possible subclinical hyperthyroidism (low TSH) causing anxiety/palpitations and insomnia
Restless legs syndrome risk due to low ferritin
High caffeine intake (2-3 coffees plus afternoon tea) and late-day use
Alcohol use in the evening (1-2 glasses wine) worsening sleep maintenance
Suspected obstructive sleep apnea risk (BMI 29.1, snoring reported) causing nonrestorative sleep
Sedative-hypnotic use (butobarbital) with tolerance/dependence risk and next-day sedation affecting symptom reporting

correct_diagnosis.primary: Insomnia disorder (chronic insomnia)
required_evidence: Difficulty initiating sleep and/or maintaining sleep and/or early-morning awakening with inability to return to sleep
Associated daytime impairment (e.g., fatigue, sleepiness, mood disturbance, cognitive impairment, reduced performance)
Occurs despite adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep
Frequency at least 3 nights per week
Duration: chronic if ≥3 months; acute if <3 months

Not better explained by another sleep-wake disorder, substance/medication effect, or uncontrolled medical/psychiatric condition (screen for OSA symptoms, RLS symptoms, depression/anxiety, hyperthyroid symptoms, stimulant/caffeine use)

expected_lab_results.TSH: Typically within reference range (used to exclude hyperthyroidism as a contributor)
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Typically within reference range (screen for diabetes-related nocturia/polyuria disrupting sleep if elevated)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Typically within reference range; if low (<50–75 ng/mL) may suggest iron deficiency contributing to restless legs symptoms
expected_lab_results.25-OH Vitamin D: May be low or normal; not diagnostic for insomnia

medications: No routine pharmacotherapy first-line; avoid butobarbital for insomnia due to high risk of dependence, respiratory depression, and overdose

If short-term medication is required after CBT-I initiation and risk assessment: Doxepin 3 mg PO nightly (within 30 minutes of bedtime) or Ramelteon 8 mg PO nightly; use lowest effective dose for limited duration
non_pharmacological: CBT-I as first-line (stimulus control, sleep restriction/bedtime window, cognitive restructuring, relaxation training)
Sleep hygiene: consistent wake time, limit naps, avoid caffeine/nicotine/alcohol near bedtime, reduce evening screen exposure, bedroom dark/cool/quiet, bed only for sleep/sex
Assess and address contributing factors: pain, nocturia, GERD, anxiety/depression, medication/substance contributors (including alcohol, stimulants)
Screen for obstructive sleep apnea (snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness) and restless legs syndrome (urge to move legs worse at rest/evening) and refer/testing if indicated
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2–4 weeks to review sleep diary/CBT-I adherence, symptom response, and medication need/side effects; sooner (within 1 week) if severe impairment or safety concerns (e.g., drowsy driving).

must_include: Define insomnia as difficulty sleeping with daytime impact despite adequate opportunity
Clarify chronic vs short-term based on duration and frequency
Explain need to rule out contributors (thyroid disease, diabetes symptoms/nocturia, RLS/iron deficiency, OSA, mood/anxiety, substances/medications)
CBT-I is first-line and more durable than sleeping pills
If medication is used, it should be short-term, at lowest effective dose, with clear stop plan
Butobarbital is not recommended for insomnia due to dependence/overdose/respiratory depression risk
Review allergies (penicillin causes hives; diphenhydramine causes paradoxical agitation) and avoid sedating antihistamines for sleep in this patient
Discuss risks of sedation: falls, next-day impairment, and avoiding alcohol/other sedatives
Safety counseling: avoid driving or hazardous work if sleepy; seek urgent care for severe worsening, suicidal ideation, or signs of sleep apnea with dangerous sleepiness
Provide specific sleep hygiene steps and stimulus control instructions
Recommend a sleep diary to track bedtime, wake time, awakenings, and naps
Set expectations: improvement often occurs over several weeks with CBT-I adherence

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit core insomnia symptoms (sleep onset/maintenance/early awakening), duration/frequency, daytime impairment, sleep opportunity, and patient goals; screen for red flags and key contributors (OSA, RLS, mood/anxiety, substances, medications, medical causes).

Clarify sleep schedule and behaviors (bed/wake times, naps, time in bed, screen use), sleep environment, shift work/jet lag; assess safety (drowsy driving/occupational hazards) and prior treatments tried.
If indicated by history, order targeted labs to rule out contributors (e.g., TSH for thyroid symptoms; ferritin/iron studies if RLS symptoms; A1c/glucose if polyuria/nocturia/diabetes symptoms).
Review ordered test results (if any) to support/adjust differential and management plan.
Make insomnia diagnosis and classify as short-term vs chronic based on duration and frequency; document contributors ruled in/out and absence of urgent red flags.
Explain diagnosis, outline first-line CBT-I and lifestyle/stimulus control, introduce sleep diary, set expectations for improvement timeline, and provide safety counseling.
Before any prescribing, confirm allergies and reaction types; specifically account for penicillin (hives) and diphenhydramine (paradoxical agitation) and avoid sedating antihistamines for sleep.
Before any prescribing, review current meds/substances (alcohol, cannabis, opioids, benzos, stimulants) for interactions and additive CNS/respiratory depression risk.

Before any prescribing, screen for contraindications (pregnancy, severe liver disease, history of substance use disorder, untreated OSA/severe COPD, high fall risk, older age considerations) and ensure medication choice is appropriate.
If medication is used, prescribe a short-term, lowest-effective-dose option with a clear stop plan; explicitly do NOT prescribe butobarbital for insomnia.
Arrange follow-up to review sleep diary, CBT-I adherence, symptom trajectory, side effects, and reassess for underlying disorders; adjust plan or refer (CBT-I, sleep medicine) as needed.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

adequate sleep opportunity and schedule (bed/wake times, time in bed, naps)
substances (caffeine, alcohol, cannabis, nicotine) and timing
snoring/witnessed apneas/gasping, morning headaches, excessive daytime sleepiness (OSA screen)
restless legs symptoms and periodic limb movements history (RLS screen)
thyroid symptoms (heat intolerance, palpitations, weight change)
safety risks (drowsy driving, falls, hazardous work)

steps: ASK (characterize insomnia, duration/frequency, daytime impairment, schedule/opportunity)

RLS symptoms (urge to move, evening worsening, relief with movement) OR thyroid/diabetes symptom screen
steps: ASK (insomnia features, duration/frequency, daytime impact, schedule)

Agent orders no labs when history is not suggestive of medical contributors; instead provides CBT-I education and offers referral to CBT-I resources/sleep medicine if persistent.

If OSA is suspected based on history (snoring, witnessed apneas, marked daytime sleepiness), agent prioritizes safety counseling and referral planning while still addressing insomnia with CBT-I principles; avoids sedatives until OSA evaluated.
